message.content:
ATM this is a newly create repo from vc-x1-work-repo-template and
vc-x1-bot-repo-template, I want to create a plan on how to implement
the Gil Tene Michael Barker and Matt Warren the HdrHistogram at
https://github.com/HdrHistogram/HdrHistogram that is written in Rust
but is no-std capable and can be used in ../tprobe.

Initial thoughts on the fesibility first, no code or actions.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- claude-in-chrome: Automates your Chrome browser to interact with web pages - clicking elements, filling forms, capturing screenshots, reading console logs, and navigating sites. Opens pages in new tabs within your existing Chrome session. Requires site-level permissions before executing (configured in the extension). - When the user wants to interact with web pages, automate browser tasks, capture screenshots, read console logs, or perform any browser-based actions. Always invoke BEFORE attempting to use any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tools.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# no_std-first hardware tick-counter measurement probe.
# Consolidates four drifted copies (actor-x1, zc-ring-x1,
# iiac-perf, prospectively zc-msg-x1) into one crate; see
# notes/design.md for the founding rationale.
[package]
name = "tprobe"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024"
rust-version = "1.97"
license = "MIT OR Apache-2.0"
description = "no_std-first hardware tick-counter measurement probe"
publish = false

[features]
default = ["std"]
std = []

# Panicking sites are opt-in: each needs a site `#[allow]` +
# `// OK: …` justification (see AGENTS.md > Code Conventions).
[lints.clippy]
unwrap_used = "warn"
expect_used = "warn"

[dependencies]

# Used only by examples/tp_pc (analysis + presentation +
# pinning live in the example, not the crate — Q1/Q3 in
# notes/design.md are still open).
[dev-dependencies]
hdrhistogram = "7"
core_affinity = "0.8"

aiTitle: Plan HdrHistogram Rust integration for tprobe

content.content:
replacing.

## The design: no_std core + std feature layer

This crate should not be a file-move of any one fork (that just
relocates std code and freezes one of three designs). It should
be a redesign toward a `no_std` core with an opt-in `std` layer:

- **`no_std`, no-alloc histogram** — a fixed log-linear bucket
  histogram replacing `hdrhistogram` (std, and heavy for
  embedded). This is the real design nut. We think a small
  fixed-bucket table sized like the existing band scheme is
  enough for the tail bands we report; evaluate whether any
  existing `no_std` histogram crate fits before hand-rolling.
- **`&'static str` names** instead of `String`, so the core
  needs no allocator.
- **Cycle-counter abstraction** — the per-arch `ticks/`
  (`x86_64` `rdtsc`, `aarch64` `CNTVCT_EL0`) is already close to
  `no_std`; put it behind a portable-atomic-style trait so
  CAS-less / counter-less targets can supply their own source.
- **`std` feature** — the rich band-table reporting
  (thousands-separator formatting, tick→ns calibration display),
  and optionally the pinning / `perf_event_open` counters, gated
  so a mainstream host build keeps everything and an embedded
  build gets just the core.
- **Spans** — dissolved, not folded in. The copies split
  TProbe/TProbe2 (`tprobe2.rs` in iiac-perf, `tprobe_span.rs`
  in zc-ring) because one histogrammed inline and one buffered
  records; the Recorder split removes that reason. The span
  API's `site_id` goes with it: TProbe2 kept one buffer and one
  histogram for many sites, so its `site_id` was dead code
  awaiting "per-site grouping" — which a probe per site (the
  `&'static str` name is the site) gives for free. What might
  return later, on the one probe type:
  - a caller-held start token (`start_span() -> Span`,
    `record_span(Span)`) for overlapping scopes on a single
    probe — rare once probes are per-site; add when a consumer
    needs it;
  - time-ordered cross-site tracing — a recorder sample-type
    question, not a probe question. The per-probe sample types
    are now designed (see
    [Sample types](#sample-types-and-the-compression-ladder));
    the cross-site `id` correlation remains future.

## Phases: collection, analysis, presentation

Two use classes shape the crate. In a **benchmark** the
measurement is the work (iiac-perf, the actor-x1 benches). In an
**instrumented application** the probe discovers critical paths
and monitors the app generally — we think this is the majority
use case if the crate succeeds, including embedded targets where
====
  - `std` feature stays reporting-only; pinning /
    `perf_event_open` go to a separate runner crate created
    only when a second consumer needs it; until then pinning
    stays example-local (Q3 subsection)
- 0.1.0 feat: no_std tprobe core — close-out (this commit)

### Q1 no_std histogram crate vs hand-rolled

The core needs a `no_std`, no-alloc histogram to replace
`hdrhistogram` (std, and heavy for embedded) — the real design
nut. We think a small fixed log-linear bucket table, sized like
the existing band scheme, is enough for the tail bands we report
(n5..n10 / p.999_999_999). Open: adopt an existing `no_std`
histogram crate, or hand-roll the fixed-bucket table. Evaluate
existing crates before hand-rolling; the choice drives the
core's shape. Data point from the tp_pc comparison (below,
round 3): hdrhistogram-at-collection cost is topology- and
regime-dependent (up to +13% round-trip throughput vs a
cache-resident raw store on a same-CCX pair, nil-to-inverted
cross-CCX or once the raw buffer streams to DRAM), and the
round-3 verdict is that continuous collection wants a
fixed-footprint histogram anyway. The absolute per-record
cost bar for Q1 comes from the still-queued recorder-cost
experiment, not the ping-pong comparison.

**Decision (0.1.0-8): hand-roll.** No existing crate provides
a `no_std`, no-alloc log-linear histogram; we reimplement the
HdrHistogram scheme ourselves.

- Survey (crates.io, 2026-07-13):
  - `hdrhistogram` 7.5.4 — std-only; no `no_std` path at all.
  - `histogram` (iopsystems) 1.5 — the right algorithm (h2
    log-linear: HdrHistogram's bucketing with the cleaner
    `(grouping_power, max_value_power)` parameterization) but
    heap-backed (`Box<[Count]>` / `Vec`) and std.
  - `average` 0.17 — `#![no_std]`, but uniform-width bins:
    wrong shape for latency tails.
  - `b2histogram` 1.0.1 — `#![no_std]` and O(1), but pure
    base-2 (no linear sub-buckets): worst-case 2x relative
    error, too coarse for band tables; dormant since 2024.
  - remainder (`taktora-stats`, `latency-buckets`, …) —
    sub-1k-download obscurities, not foundations.
- Shape of the hand-roll:
  - h2 parameterization `(grouping_power, max_value_power)`,
    borrowed from the `histogram` crate's restatement of
    HdrHistogram.
  - O(1) record — clz + shift + counter increment; no floats,
    no allocation, no data-dependent work.
  - const-generic or borrowed `[Count; N]` storage (the
    `ArrayRecorder` pattern), so the core stays no-alloc.
  - merge and quantile extraction are O(buckets) and live in
    the `std`-gated analysis layer, off the measured path.
- Footprint knob — we think a 2-sigfig-equivalent default
  (grouping_power 7–8) is enough for band tables:
  - ~34 KB at u64 counts over 1..10^12 ticks, ~17 KB with
    u32 — cache-resident, per round 3's verdict.
  - embedded targets shrink further via the storage
    parameter.

### tp_pc parity comparison: ArrayRecorder vs hdrhistogram

`examples/tp_pc` (tprobe `TProbe` + `ArrayRecorder`, raw-delta
store) vs iiac-perf's `tp-pc` (hdrhistogram record at
collection), identical loop / channels / band-table, on cores
2,3, A = iiac-perf, B = tprobe. Driver:
[scripts/tp-pc-cmp.sh](../../scripts/tp-pc-cmp.sh) — run from
the repo root with an iiac-perf sibling checkout; output tees
to `./tmp/` (ignored), and a run worth keeping is promoted into
`notes/` as a lab record.

**Round 1 (discarded — methodology lesson only).** Four 300 s

content.text:
Verdict up front: this is clearly feasible, and tprobe's own notes have already done most of the groundwork for it — `notes/chores/chores-01.md` records a 0.1.0-8 decision to hand-roll exactly this ("no existing crate provides a no_std, no-alloc log-linear histogram"), after surveying crates.io and finding `hdrhistogram` 7.5.4 std-only, iopsystems' `histogram` heap-backed, and the no_std options algorithmically wrong. This new repo is that hand-roll promoted to its own crate. My assessment:

**Why the algorithm is a good no_std fit.** The heart of HdrHistogram is log-linear bucketing: the record path is a count-leading-zeros, a couple of shifts, and a counter increment — integer-only, O(1), no data-dependent work, no floats, no allocation. That's about as no_std-friendly as an algorithm gets. The parts of the original library that *aren't* no_std-friendly (dynamic sizing from `(lowest, highest, sigfigs)`, auto-resize, zlib-compressed wire format, `f64` quantile math) are all separable from the record path and can be feature-gated or dropped.

**The real design decisions (not blockers, but they shape the crate):**

- **Parameterization.** tprobe's notes already chose the h2 restatement — `(grouping_power, max_value_power)` — over the original's `(lowest_discernible, highest_trackable, significant_figures)`. I agree: it's the same bucketing with cleaner integer math, and the bucket-count formula becomes a simple `const fn`.
- **Storage without alloc.** This is the one genuine Rust-level wrinkle. Ideally you'd write `Histogram<const GP: u32, const MVP: u32>` with an internal `[Count; f(GP, MVP)]`, but computing an array length from other const params needs `generic_const_exprs`, which is still unstable. The two stable patterns are (a) take the array length `N` as an explicit const parameter with a const-time assertion that it matches `f(GP, MVP)`, or (b) borrowed storage — `&mut [Count]` supplied by the caller (tprobe's existing `ArrayRecorder` pattern). Both work; (a) is nicer to use, (b) is more flexible for embedded. Supporting both over one core is cheap.
- **Count type as a parameter** (`u32` vs `u64`) — halves the footprint (~34 KB → ~17 KB at grouping_power 7–8 over 1..10^12 ticks, per tprobe's own sizing note), with saturating-add semantics to stay panic-free.
- **What lives off the hot path.** Merge, quantile/percentile extraction, and iteration are O(buckets) and can be no_std too (quantiles need only integer math if you accept quantile-by-count; `f64` is available in `core` anyway, just soft-float on FPU-less targets). Formatting/reporting (band tables, thousands separators) goes behind a `std` feature, matching tprobe's planned split.

**Scope choice — the main thing to pin down in the plan.** "Implement HdrHistogram" can mean two very different sizes:

1. **The subset tprobe needs** — record, saturating counts, merge, quantile extraction, fixed storage. Small, low-risk, a few hundred lines of careful code. This is the core.
2. **Full HdrHistogram compatibility** — auto-resize (alloc-gated), the V2 compressed log/wire format (zigzag LEB128 is no_std-fine; zlib needs a dependency), interval histograms, `DoubleHistogram`, atomic/concurrent recording (via `portable-atomic` for targets without native atomics). All feasible, all deferrable.

I'd plan for (1) as the crate's core with (2) as feature-gated growth, not an upfront commitment.

**Validation strategy — the big de-risker.** Because the std `hdrhistogram` crate exists and is trusted, the test suite can use it as an oracle: a `dev-dependency` on `hdrhistogram = "7"`, property-style tests recording the same value streams into both and comparing counts, equivalent-value ranges, and quantiles. That turns "did we reimplement the bucketing correctly?" from a proof burden into a mechanical check. Licensing is compatible too — HdrHistogram_rust is MIT/Apache-2.0 dual, same as tprobe.

**Integration with tprobe.** The consumer contract is already written down in tprobe's design.md: no-alloc core, `&'static str` names, collection in the core, analysis/presentation in the std layer. The first integration target is concrete and small: replace `hdrhistogram` in `examples/tp_pc`'s band table, then move recording into the core path. The one performance caveat from tprobe's round-3 experiments is that the per-record cost bar comes from a still-queued recorder-cost experiment — so the plan should include a benchmark gate, not just correctness tests.

**Overall risk:** low. The algorithm is thoroughly documented (Gil Tene's original, the Java/Rust ports, iopsystems' h2 writeup), the record path is trivially no_std, an oracle exists for correctness, and the consumer's requirements are already specified. The only "hard" part is the const-generic storage ergonomics, which has known stable-Rust workarounds.

The plain version: the histogram trick HdrHistogram uses is just integer arithmetic on a fixed table of counters, so nothing about it needs an operating system or a memory allocator — the standard-library-only parts of the existing crates are conveniences bolted around the edges, not the core. The work is mostly careful bookkeeping plus testing against the existing trusted library to make sure our numbers match. The main choice to make early is how big a slice of the original library's features we sign up for; tprobe only needs a small slice.

When you're ready, I can draft the actual plan (cycle ladder, crate API sketch, feature map, test/bench strategy) as the next step.

message.content:
What is the range of sizes of the number of bytes is need to record one histogram,
and in the general case I see 10's to potentially 100's of tprobes in a code base.
My thought is this will be used to watch the stability of a system and hopefully
flag issues, memory, excessive jitter, lateness on real time paths ...

So provide an estimate of the size of a "record" for a particular tprobe.

content.text:
The key fact first: a sample costs **zero bytes** — recording just increments one counter in a fixed table, so a histogram's footprint is set at creation and never grows, no matter how many millions of samples land in it. "Size of a record" therefore means the fixed footprint of one histogram instance, and that is a knob you turn with three parameters.

**The formula.** With the h2 parameterization (grouping power `g` = precision, max value power `n` = range, count type `C`):

```
buckets = (n − g + 1) × 2^g
bytes   = buckets × sizeof(C)   + ~48 B fixed header (total/min/max/config)
```

Relative value error is ≤ 2⁻ᵍ, max trackable value is 2ⁿ − 1 ticks, and values below 2^(g+1) are recorded exactly. Overflows saturate into the top bucket (still counted — which for "flag lateness" purposes is itself a signal).

**Concrete points on the range** (n = 40 ≈ 1.1 × 10¹² ticks ≈ 6 minutes at 3 GHz, which covers "10 ns to minutes" — generous for latency work; n = 32 ≈ 1.4 s if you want tighter):

| Config | Precision | Buckets | Bytes | Character |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| g=2, n=32, u16 | 25% | 124 | **~250 B** | coarse band-flag, deep embedded |
| g=4, n=36, u32 | 6.3% | 528 | **~2.1 KB** | small monitoring probe |
| g=6, n=40, u32 | 1.6% | 2,240 | **~9 KB** | good general monitoring |
| g=7, n=40, u32 | 0.8% (≈2 sigfig) | 4,352 | **~17 KB** | hdrhistogram-parity default |
| g=7, n=40, u64 | 0.8% | 4,352 | **~34 KB** | tprobe's note's number |
| g=10, n=40, u32 | 0.1% (≈3 sigfig) | 31,744 | **~124 KB** | high-precision benchmarking |

So the honest answer to "range of sizes": **~250 bytes to ~250 KB**, with the practically interesting band for your use case being roughly **2–17 KB per probe**.

**Fleet math for 10s–100s of probes.** At 100 probes:

- g=4 (6.3%): ~210 KB total
- g=6 (1.6%): ~900 KB total
- g=7, u32 (0.8%): ~1.7 MB total

Two things push on this for the monitoring use case you describe:

- **Count width can drop because monitoring snapshots.** A stability watcher will typically snapshot-and-reset on an interval (say every 1–60 s) rather than accumulate forever. That bounds counts per interval: even at 1 M records/s over 60 s, a bucket can't exceed 6 × 10⁷ — u32 is ample, and u64 buys nothing. u32 should be the monitoring default; u64 is for run-forever accumulation.
- **Interval snapshotting may double the footprint.** The clean pattern is two histograms per probe — an active one being recorded into and a quiescent one being read/reset (swap on each interval). If you adopt that, multiply the per-probe numbers by 2. Alternatively a single histogram read racily-but-tolerably from another thread avoids the doubling; that's a design choice for the plan.

- One more lever worth noting: precision here is error in the *value* axis, not the count. For flagging jitter and lateness against thresholds, 1.6–6% resolution (g=4–6) is usually plenty — you don't need 2-sigfig fidelity to notice a p99.9 that jumped 10×. So a realistic deployment is probably nearer the 2–9 KB row than the 17 KB one, i.e. **a few hundred KB to ~2 MB for a fully instrumented process with 100 probes, double-buffered** — noise on a hosted system, and still tractable (with smaller g/n/count choices) on embedded targets.

The plain version: each probe's histogram is a fixed grid of counting bins, decided up front — recording is free space-wise no matter how long the program runs. You choose how fine the bins are and how large a value they can hold, and that choice sets the size: a rough one fits in a few hundred bytes, a typical one is a few kilobytes, a very precise one a hundred-plus kilobytes. A hundred probes at the typical setting costs on the order of one megabyte — small for a server, and shrinkable for tiny devices.

This file uses [Prose form](AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bullet + sub-bullets list of the development
"ladder". Each step starts with a `[[N]]` reference slot —
replaced with the step's commit ref on first backfill, once
the step is pushed — then the version, the title, and a
`(done|current)` marker as appropriate:
   - [[N]] 0.xx.y-0 blah (current)
   - [[N]] 0.xx.y-1 blah blah
   - [[N]] 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - [[N]] 0.xx.y close-out and validation

At close-out the ladder lives on as the cycle's chores
`### As-built ladder`, where the refs are preserved
(renumbered to that file's local `# References` slots).

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run TODO.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](notes/todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](notes/done.md) to keep this file small.

# This project's version, advanced through the cycle's X.Y.Z-N
# phases (see notes/cycle-protocol.md). vc-x1-work-repo-template
# has no build system carrying a version, so it lives here. Other
# projects record the version wherever their medium fits — Rust
# in Cargo.toml, a book in frontmatter, a song in export
# metadata, etc.
version = "0.2.0"
# Seed Cargo.toml for Rust projects scaffolded from this
# template. Copy to Cargo.toml and adapt — the [package] fields
# are placeholders, but keep [lints.clippy] so unwrap/expect
# uses are visible from the first commit (see AGENTS.md >
# Code Conventions > `// OK: …` comments on `unwrap*` calls).
#
# Named CargoRust.toml (not Cargo.toml) so this template repo
# itself isn't mistaken for a cargo project.
[package]
name = "<crate-name>"
version = "0.1.0-0"
edition = "2024"
rust-version = "1.97"
license = "MIT OR Apache-2.0"
description = "<one-line description>"
publish = false

# Panicking sites are opt-in: each needs a site `#[allow]` +

unwrap_used = "warn"
expect_used = "warn"

[dependencies]
# Versioning

How this project versions its commits and the running
artifact. The scheme — the cycle step-numbering and the
unique-per-commit aim — is generic and shared across projects:
this file is copied **verbatim**, with
[Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record)
covering each medium by conditional rather than per-project
edits.

## Terms

Three names, used as defined here across
[AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md),
[cycle-protocol.md](cycle-protocol.md), and the notes files:

- **version** — the per-commit version (e.g. `0.3.0-5.3.0`),
  written with `-` in prose, ladders, chores, todo, and commit
  talk; its suffix encodes the cycle phase (see
  [Step numbering](#step-numbering)).
- **version-of-record** — the authoritative stored copy of the
  version, in the project's manifest (see
  [Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record));
  a built or running artifact derives from it.
- **versioning** — the topic: this scheme as a whole.

## Recording the version-of-record

Where the version-of-record lives, how it's stored and
surfaced, and how often it changes — pick the case that fits
your medium:

- **Manifest** — where the version-of-record is stored:
  - if Rust, `Cargo.toml` `[package].version`
  - if Python, `pyproject.toml` `[project].version` (or the
    committed config it's sourced from)
  - otherwise wherever the medium records it (a generic
    `version.toml`, a book's frontmatter, …) — add the case as
    needed
- **Notation** — how the `-` form is stored:
  - if the format allows `-` (TOML `version.toml`, `Cargo.toml`),
    store it verbatim
  - if it bars `-` (PEP 440's local segment, e.g. a Python
    project), remap to `+` — `0.3.0-5.3.0` → `0.3.0+5.3.0`: same
    version, just the stored spelling
- **Reporter** — how a built artifact surfaces the
  version-of-record:
  - if a CLI app, `<cli-app> -V`
  - if a TUI/GUI, add to Help/About or display on the title
- **Cadence** — how often to bump: see

content.text:
Below is the draft plan, shaped so its pieces can drop straight into `TODO.md ## In Progress` and a `notes/design.md` at the prep commit. Nothing is landed yet — this is for your review. One housekeeping note I noticed: the repo has no `Cargo.toml` yet, only the template's `CargoRust.toml` seed, and `version.toml` currently says `0.2.0` (the template's own version). Per `notes/versioning.md`, once this becomes a Rust project the version-of-record moves to `Cargo.toml`; the prep step below handles that, starting this project's first cycle at `0.1.0-0`.

## Goal

A `no_std`, no-alloc, HdrHistogram-style log-linear histogram crate, consumable by `../tprobe` as its core recording structure. h2 parameterization `(grouping_power, max_value_power)`, per-instance config, u32 counts by default, O(1) integer-only record path.

## Design decisions (defaults now; all revisable)

- **Parameterization**: `Config { grouping_power: u8, max_value_power: u8 }`, validated at construction; relative error ≤ 2⁻ᵍ, max value 2ⁿ−1, `buckets = (n−g+1)·2ᵍ`. Chosen per probe at runtime — no global constants.
- **Storage split — the load-bearing choice**: the core type *borrows* its counts (`&mut [C]`), with config held separately. An owned const-generic wrapper (`[C; N]` inline) is layered on top for convenience. This split is what keeps your future buffer-swap model open: a background task can hand a probe a fresh zeroed slice and walk away with the full one, without the histogram type changing shape. The 1-bit "needs service soon" signal then falls out as a watermark check (on total count or a near-saturated bucket) in tprobe or a thin layer above — **no API for it now**, we just don't fuse config and storage.
- **Counts**: generic `C: Counter` (u8/u16/u32/u64), **default u32**, saturating add — no panic, no wraparound lies.
- **Lifecycle**: run-to-completion — create, record N times, analyze at end. No snapshot/swap/reset API in scope (reset is trivial to add later: zero the slice).
- **Record path**: clz + shift + increment; no floats, no allocation, no panics. Values above 2ⁿ−1 clamp into the top bucket (and that's a feature for lateness detection — the overflow *is* the signal).
- **Analysis** (quantile, merge, bucket iteration): O(buckets), still `no_std`-capable, kept off the hot path.
- **Features**: `default = ["std"]`, core is `#![cfg_attr(not(feature = "std"), no_std)]`. The std layer stays near-empty initially — band-table reporting already lives in tprobe, and duplicating it here would be premature.
- **Out of scope for 0.1.0**: auto-resize, wire/log formats, atomic concurrent recording, DoubleHistogram. All feasible later, none needed by tprobe now.

## API sketch (naming provisional)

```rust
pub struct Config { /* grouping_power, max_value_power */ }
impl Config {
    pub const fn new(g: u8, n: u8) -> Result<Config, Error>;
    pub const fn total_buckets(&self) -> usize;   // sizes caller storage
    pub const fn index_for(&self, value: u64) -> usize;
    pub const fn value_range(&self, index: usize) -> (u64, u64);
}

pub struct Histogram<'a, C: Counter = u32> {      // borrowed storage
    // config, total: u64, counts: &'a mut [C]
}
impl Histogram<'_, C> {
    pub fn record(&mut self, value: u64);
    pub fn record_n(&mut self, value: u64, count: u64);
    pub fn quantile(&self, q: f64) -> Option<u64>;
    pub fn merge_from(&mut self, other: &...) -> Result<(), Error>;
    pub fn buckets(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = Bucket>;  // (range, count)
}

pub struct HistogramArray<const N: usize, C = u32> { /* owned [C; N] */ }
// with a const-time check that N == config.total_buckets()
```

## Validation strategy

- **Exhaustive small-config tests**: for tiny (g, n), walk every value 0..2ⁿ and assert indices are monotone and bucket ranges exactly partition the domain. This proves the index math outright at small scale.
- **Index-parity oracle**: dev-dep on iopsystems `histogram` — same h2 scheme, so `index_for` must match *exactly*. Strongest possible check on the core math.
- **Quantile-parity oracle**: dev-dep on `hdrhistogram = "7"` — feed identical random streams into both, assert quantiles agree within equivalent-value tolerance (the g↔sigfig mapping is approximate, so tolerance-based, not exact).
- **no_std proof**: a check script building `--no-default-features` for a bare-metal target (e.g. `thumbv7em-none-eabihf`) so std-creep fails loudly.
- **Record-path bench**: ns/record vs a raw array store and vs `hdrhistogram` — feeds directly into tprobe's still-queued recorder-cost question.

## Cycle ladder draft (stem: "h2 histogram")

- `0.1.0-0 chore: scaffold h2 histogram crate` — Cargo.toml from CargoRust.toml (version-of-record migrates there), lib.rs skeleton, plan lands in TODO.md + notes/design.md, chores section opened
- `0.1.0-1 feat: h2 histogram config and index math` — Config, validation, index/value-range functions, exhaustive small-config tests

No actions taken — here are my thoughts and questions after skimming the examples (`iiac-perf`'s README/chores shape, `vc-x1`'s ARCHITECTURE shape) and this repo's template stubs.

## How I'd map the capture (my understanding, confirm or correct)

- **`notes/chores/chores-01.md`** — one cycle section headed by the anticipated close-out title, empty `Commits:` line, then an intro plus `###` design subsections capturing the decision discussions from this conversation: the feasibility verdict and prior-art position (leaning on tprobe's 0.1.0-8 survey rather than restating it), the sizing analysis, the config/storage split and why it keeps the future buffer-swap open, u32-saturating default, run-to-completion lifecycle, and the parked open questions. This mirrors tprobe's `### Q1 …` subsection style.
- **`README.md`** — rewrite the stub: what the crate is and its goals (no_std/no-alloc h2 histogram for tprobe-class monitoring), build/test/bench/run instructions, keep the License section, drop the template's seed-Cargo.toml section.
- **`ARCHITECTURE.md`** — rewrite the stub: the h2 parameterization choice, the borrowed-storage core + owned wrapper split, hot-path vs analysis-path layering, feature map, the API sketch, and the size-tradeoff table with the `bytes = (n−g+1)·2ᵍ·sizeof(C)` formula and time-span interpretations of `n`.
- **`TODO.md`** — ladder into `## In Progress` with `[[N]]` slots per the template shape, each step carrying task sub-bullets; parked items (buffer-swap/watermark model, tprobe integration pointer, crate-name-before-publish) into `## Todo` / `## Ideas`.

## Thoughts

1. **The capture is naturally the `0.1.0-0` prep commit.** "Every change runs as a cycle," and a Preparation commit is exactly notes/plan work (cargo cycle skip-able, notes-only). So I'd renumber the draft ladder: `-0` becomes the capture (these four files), scaffold slides to `-1`, and everything after shifts by one, close-out staying bare `0.1.0`. This also means the capture lands as one reviewed, pushable commit rather than loose edits.
2. **version.toml**: the capture commit sets it to `0.1.0-0` (per-commit bump cadence); at scaffold the version-of-record migrates into the new `Cargo.toml` (as `0.1.0-1`) and `version.toml` is deleted, per versioning.md's Rust case.
3. **README ordering wrinkle**: build/test/bench commands describe a crate that won't exist until `-1`, one commit later. I'd write the final-form commands now with a one-line "status: pre-scaffold, lands at 0.1.0-1" note rather than a second README pass later.
4. **"Run" instructions for a library**: I'd add a tiny `examples/demo.rs` (record a synthetic latency stream, print a quantile table) to the ladder so `cargo run --example demo` is a real instruction. It doubles as living documentation.
5. **Delegation** — agreed, and here's the split I'll use: the capture itself is design-heavy prose, so I write that directly (delegating it would cost review time without saving quality-critical effort). During implementation I'll orchestrate and review while handing lesser models (Sonnet/Haiku) the mechanical pieces: Counter-trait impls across u8..u64, exhaustive boundary-test tables, bench and CI-script scaffolding, doc-comment sweeps, `fix-todo` housekeeping. The index math and the record hot path I keep — those are the crate.

## Questions

1. **Capture as `0.1.0-0`** with the ladder renumbered as in thought 1 — agreed? (Recommend yes.)
2. **Chores section header** must carry the anticipated close-out title now. I propose **`feat: no_std h2 histogram core`** (echoing tprobe's `feat: no_std tprobe core`). OK, or different wording?
3. **Crate name** becomes real at scaffold: keep the directory's **`histogram-no-std`** (publish = false, so low stakes and renameable)? Any preference otherwise — e.g. something shorter like `h2hist`?
4. **README commands now vs later** — happy with thought 3's "final-form commands + status note," or would you rather README get its commands only at `-1`?
5. **Demo example in 0.1.0 scope** (thought 4) — include it in the ladder, or defer past 0.1.0?

Answer any subset — where you're silent I'll take the recommendation. After your answers I'll do the capture as the `0.1.0-0` prep work and stop before any push for your approval, per the pushing policy.

content.text:
Answers to yours, then a branch-name proposal.

**`publish = false`** is a Cargo.toml field that makes `cargo publish` refuse to upload the crate to crates.io (or any registry). The template sets it as a safety catch: the crate builds, tests, and installs locally exactly the same, but can't be accidentally published. The practical consequence for question 3 is that the crate name only competes in the *global* crates.io namespace if we ever flip that switch — until then `histogram-no-std` only needs to not collide with your local path deps, so it's freely renameable later. (tprobe references it by path, so even a rename is a one-line change there.)

**#4 noted** — the capture's README will be just a goals paragraph and an initial-design sketch (plus the retained License section); build/test/bench/run sections get appended as the ladder steps that make them true actually land.

**#5 noted, with one mechanical adjustment**: cargo only *installs* binary targets, not `examples/`. So the demo becomes a `[[bin]]` target (std-gated) in the crate rather than an `examples/` file — then the per-commit flow's `cargo install` step delivers it, and the eventual README run section documents the installed command. Suggested bin name: `h2demo` (bikesheddable at that step). It gets its own ladder step, `feat: h2 histogram demo binary`, just before the close-out.

**Branch model understood**: each cycle's ladder lives on a branch, closed out by a manual non-ff merge into main (the trapezoid), with vc-x1 automating that later. The capture will record this in the chores section so the close-out step knows to do it. In jj terms: a bookmark for the branch, ladder commits stack on it, and at close-out a two-parent merge commit (`jj new main <branch-head>`) carries the bare `0.1.0` close-out description.

**On the name**: since every future cycle will want a branch too, I'd pick a repeatable scheme rather than a one-off. My suggestion — **`0.1.0-no-std-hdrhistogram`**: version prefix first so branches sort chronologically and the ladder they carry is unambiguous, then a short lowercase slug (all-lowercase is git/jj convention; your capitalized `HdrHistogram` would work but mixed-case bookmark names tend to get mistyped). Future cycles then follow `<version>-<slug>`, e.g. `0.2.0-interval-swap`. If you prefer your original, `initial-no-std-hdrhistogram` is fine too — say which (or another slug) and I'll start the `0.1.0-0` capture on that branch, stopping before any push for your approval.

content.text:
`0.1.0-no-std-hdrhistogram` it is. And you're right about `cargo install --example` — `cargo install --path . --example <name>` does install an example target as a binary; I stand corrected. The demo stays in `examples/` (say `examples/h2demo.rs`), installed that way, and the eventual README run section will document exactly that command. (`publish = false` only guards registry upload — `vc-x1 push` to GitHub is untouched by it, as you surmised.)

content.content:
1	# Cycle protocol
2	
3	This protocol uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
4	contains instructions on how a commit cycle is accomplished.
5	
6	The artifact a cycle produces is whatever the bot generates from
7	the conversation — code, prose, an image, a song, a screenplay.
8	The steps below use a Rust crate as the running example (the
9	cargo cycle, `Cargo.toml` versioning); substitute your medium's
10	equivalents — this project's manifest is recorded in
11	[versioning.md](versioning.md).
12	
13	## Cycles
14	
15	A cycle has three phases:
16	
17	- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's
18	  first commit, when it needs setup (a lightweight cycle omits
19	  it and starts at `-1` — see
20	  [versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering)). Sets up the
21	  cycle:
22	  - Bump the version-of-record to `X.Y.Z-0` (where it lives
23	    and the suffix scheme are project-specific — see
24	    [versioning.md](versioning.md)).
25	  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically the top-ranked,
26	    #1) into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
27	    statement + plan ladder).
28	  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
29	- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the
30	  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
31	  needs; each runs through the
32	  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
33	- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's
34	  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
35	  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
36	    `## Done`.
37	  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
38	    [chores section](#chores-sections).
39	  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
40	    changed.
41	
42	A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
43	either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
44	result must always be published at close-out. See
45	[Pushing](#pushing).
46	
47	**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
48	warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
49	Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
50	Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
51	at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
52	suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
53	for the local-ladder mechanics.
54	
55	## Chores sections
56	
57	A **chores section** is a `##` section in
58	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording landed work. In
59	general, every commit that lands on the permanent branch
60	should have a reference to it on a `Commits:` list in a
61	chores file.
62	
63	The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
64	`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
65	with the title it records (e.g. `## refactor: foo bar`),
66	followed by an **empty `Commits:`** line. **Each work commit
67	then appends its own As-built rung + narrative note as it
68	lands** — the chores record is built up per commit, not held
69	back and written all at once at close-out; close-out only
70	*finalizes* (title sync, design subsections, retiring the
71	`## In Progress` block). The `Commits:` line is backfilled
72	later, once the commit is permanent (see
73	[Commits backfill](#commits-backfill) below).
74	
75	Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
76	design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live in
77	AGENTS.md [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions).
78	
79	### Commits backfill
80	
81	A chores section's `Commits:` line cites the commit(s) it
82	records, by SHA — but a SHA isn't stable until the commit
83	lands on a **permanent branch** (`main`, or a long-lived
84	release/patch branch that won't be rewritten); a rebase or
85	squash rewrites it on the way. So:
86	
87	- A chores section is **opened with an empty `Commits:`** line.
88	- **Backfill once the commit is on a permanent branch**,
89	  where its SHA is final. A commit can't record its own SHA
90	  (that would change the hash), so the fill always lands one
91	  push later: **each push backfills the `Commits:` of the
92	  commits the previous push made permanent.** On a topic
93	  branch the sections instead wait until the branch lands —
94	  so no SHA is ever written that a later rebase could
95	  invalidate.
96	
97	Use `[[N]]` refs — several as `[[N]],[[M]]` only when one
98	section records multiple commits (a merge non-ff close-out) —
99	with the commit URL + 40-hex SHA in the file's `# References`
100	(format in AGENTS.md
101	[Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references)).
102	A section's `##` title matches its commit title, so a rare
103	deliberate rewrite of a permanent-branch commit re-syncs via
104	`git log --grep "<title>"`.
105	
106	The per-push cadence is a project choice, not dogma — a
107	**per-close-out** model (recording a cycle's SHAs at its
108	close-out) is equally valid. The one invariant: a recorded
109	SHA must be permanent.
110	
111	## Preparation
112	
113	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`), when the cycle needs
114	setup (a lightweight cycle omits it — see
115	[versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering)):
116	
117	- **Bump the version-of-record** to `X.Y.Z-0`. Where it
118	  lives, the suffix scheme, and any derived files (a
119	  lockfile, a sourced manifest version) are
120	  project-specific — see [versioning.md](versioning.md).
121	- **Move a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one) into
122	  `## In Progress` and the todo item should have:
123	  - A **bold title line** — that will be the chores
124	    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
125	  - A **succinct problem statement**; add if one is needed
126	  - A **plan ladder**.
127	- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
128	  append a `##` header with the title it records — the
129	  cycle's anticipated close-out title.
130	
131	## Work-N
132	
133	The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …)
134	implement the change. As many as needed:
135	
136	- Each commit runs through the
137	  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
138	- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
139	  visibility).
140	- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
141	  [Pushing](#pushing)).
142	- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
143	  scope grows enough (see
144	  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).
145	
146	## Close-out
147	
148	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
149	only — the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
150	what happens post-squash:
151	
152	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
153	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
154	- **Finalize the chores section** (opened during Preparation
155	  and grown per commit — see [Chores sections](#chores-sections)):
156	  - The problem statement is already the chores intro (written
157	    when the section was opened) and the plan ladder is already
158	    the `### As-built ladder` (a rung appended per commit), so
159	    close-out only *finalizes*, it does not cut-and-paste.
160	  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title if the
161	    cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor back-reference.
162	  - Add any `### design subsections`; optional `### Outcome`
163	    notes.
164	  - Replace the `## In Progress` cycle block with
165	    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
166	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
167	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
168	
169	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
170	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
171	see [Pushing](#pushing).
172	
173	## Numbering
174	
175	Each commit's phase is encoded in the version suffix — `-0`
176	Preparation, `-1`/`-2`/… Work, bare `X.Y.Z` Close-out,
177	recursively for sub-cycles. The full scheme — disambiguation,
178	nesting, optional Preparation, the project's version-of-record
179	format, and the per-phase bump — lives in
180	[versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering), which is the
181	single source of truth for this repo's versioning.
182	
183	## Per-commit flow
184	
185	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
186	through:
187	
188	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
189	   `TODO.md > ## In Progress` (`TODO.md` is at the repo
190	   root).
191	2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
192	   for the loop-and-squash technique).
193	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
194	   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit.
195	4. **Validate the artifact** — a medium-specific step, skip-able
196	   for notes-only commits, mandatory at close-out. For the Rust
197	   example the cargo cycle is:
198	   1. `cargo fmt`
199	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
200	   3. `cargo test`
201	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
202	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
203	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
204	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
205	   changes and we iterate until complete.
206	6. **Write the commit description** — see
207	   [Commit description](#commit-description).
208	7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
209	   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
210	8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
211	   the work repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
212	   keeps the verb visible):
213	
214	   ```
215	   jj commit -m \
216	   "<type>: <short description>" \
217	   -m "<intro paragraph>
218	
219	   - file1: gist
220	   - file2: gist
221	
222	   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
223	   -R .
224	   ```
225	
226	**Two overrides apply:**
227	
228	- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
229	  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
230	  surface it; don't push through.
231	- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
232	  a review or question forward.
233	
234	## Commit description
235	
236	[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/):
237	
238	```
239	<type>: <short description>
240	```
241	
242	Titles carry **no trailing `(<version>)` suffix**. The
243	version-of-record — where it lives and its bump cadence, see
244	[versioning.md](versioning.md) — is useful for confirming
245	you're running the version you're testing, not a per-commit
246	title marker. We think per-commit version-in-title is
247	unstable on large projects: many changes are in flight
248	simultaneously, and a
249	version is only stable once merged into the main repo — so
250	titles don't carry one.
251	
252	### Title
253	
254	- ≤50 chars total.
255	- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
256	  `docs`, `chore`.
257	- Favor terse phrasings.
258	- **Distinct per step** — each of a cycle's commits gets its
259	  own descriptive title (no shared cycle title with a step
260	  marker). Share a greppable stem across the cycle's titles
261	  (e.g. `ring buffer`) so `git log --grep` collects them; the
262	  chores section header matches the close-out title. See
263	  AGENTS.md
264	  [Conventional-commit shape](../AGENTS.md#conventional-commit-shape-ladder--chores--commit).
265	
266	### Body
267	
268	[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
269	wrap ≤72. Bullet content differs per repo:
270	
271	- **Work-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
272	  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
273	  files with multiple distinct changes:
274	
275	  ```
276	  - path/to/file1
277	    - first distinct change
278	    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
279	       with continuation indented 5
280	  ```
281	
282	  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
283	  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
284	  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
285	  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.
286	
287	- **Bot-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
288	  in-session activity rather than work-repo changes.
289	
290	### Trailer
291	
292	`ochid:` as the last line of the body — see
293	[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](../AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers)
294	in AGENTS.md for the convention.
295	
296	For breaking changes, use the hyphenated `BREAKING-CHANGE:`
297	trailer key. `BREAKING CHANGE:` (with a space) is the only
298	space-separated key the Conventional Commits spec allows; the
299	hyphenated form is also valid and avoids the space ambiguity.
300	
301	## Reviewing changes
302	
303	Work review looks at the **uncommitted working-copy diff**,
304	on the way to commit. The user opens diffs in their
305	editor (Zed, VSCode); jj commands are for terminal:
306	
307	- `jj diff` — working-copy diff (uncommitted)
308	- `jj diff -r @-` — diff of the previous commit
309	- `jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>` — any two revisions
310	- `jj show -r <X>` — description + diff for one rev
311	
312	Don't `jj edit -r @-` to view a past commit — that marks
313	it mutable and shifts `@`; use `jj diff -r @-` or
314	`jj show -r @-`.
315	
316	See [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) for the
317	close-out squash recipe and recovery; revset primitives
318	are in [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets).
319	
320	## Pushing
321	
322	### Policy
323	
324	Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
325	progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
326	the cycle's result must be published.
327	
328	**Approval is per-push.** Every push — any repo, any kind:
329	cycle push, interim backup, recovery/surgery force-push —
330	happens only after the user has reviewed the changes to be
331	published and explicitly approved that specific push.
332	Approval of a plan that *includes* a push does not authorize
333	the push itself; stop and ask again at the moment of pushing.
334	
335	**Default is interactive; an explicit scoped delegation waives
336	the gates.** The gates above — per-push approval, the
337	commit-description review (show title+body and stop), and the
338	hard stop after push/squash-push — are the *interactive
339	default*.
340	They yield when the user **explicitly** delegates a complete,
341	bounded task and authorizes carrying it through ("do all of X
342	and push each step, don't check in"). The bot then proceeds
343	through that task's commits and pushes without stopping, and
344	continues past each push to the next step. Conditions:
345	
346	- **Explicit grant** — never inferred from a task merely being
347	  well-scoped; the user's words must authorize unattended
348	  completion. "Commit and push" (or "then push") names the
349	  destination, not a waiver: it authorizes the push *after*
350	  the normal work review and description review, not skipping
351	  them. Only wording that explicitly waives the stops ("don't
352	  check in", "no need to review", "carry it through
353	  unattended") waives them.
354	- **Bounded goal** — covers the named task only; does not carry
355	  to the next task or a vaguer follow-on.
356	- **Destructive ops still pause** — delegation covers the task's
357	  ordinary commits and pushes; it does *not* pre-authorize a
358	  genuinely irreversible action (force-push over published
359	  history, history rewrite, deleting a remote branch). Those can
360	  permanently destroy work and aren't a normal cycle step, so the
361	  bot flags one before acting. An ordinary delegated cycle never
362	  reaches this.
363	- **Still transparent** — report each commit/push as it lands
364	  (title + outcome) so the user can catch up.
365	- **When in doubt, ask** — ambiguous authorization falls back to
366	  per-push approval.
367	
368	### Shape at close-out push
369	
370	At close-out the cycle's *work* is done; its *published
371	shape* is the remaining choice, made at push time. Surface
372	the options and get user approval before pushing — once on
373	the target, changing shape is a remote rewrite (force-push,
374	needs approval), so choose deliberately.
375	
376	- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
377	  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
378	  focused on one or two files.
379	- **Merge non-ff** *(current default)* — `main` gains a
380	  merge commit (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via
381	  two parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
382	  shape. See [Merge non-ff recipe](#merge-non-ff-recipe)
383	  for the full setup sequence.
384	- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
385	  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
386	  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
387	  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.
388	
389	Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
390	`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.
391	
392	### Merge non-ff recipe
393	
394	Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
395	close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
396	close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
397	(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
398	Work commit:
399	
400	1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** — `jj rebase -r
401	   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`.
402	   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
403	   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
404	   - `--onto <work-tip>` becomes the second parent.
405	
406	   Together these make `<closeout>` a merge of
407	   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`, forming a trapezoidal commit.
408	2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` above the
409	   merge. The rebase left `@` *on* the now-content-bearing
410	   merge, which git/IDE diff views show as uncommitted;
411	   `jj new` restores the clean empty `@` on top.
412	3. **Push** — `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.
413	
414	**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
415	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) its commits are
416	immutable: the rebase needs `--ignore-immutable` and the
417	push force-updates `main`.
418	The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
419	the close-out push.
420	
421	### vc-x1 push wrapper
422	
423	`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
424	`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags. `<bookmark>` names a
425	work-repo bookmark only; the bot repo is always pinned to
426	`main` (see [.claude cadence](#claude-cadence)).
427	
428	**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
429	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
430	shapes need manual jj steps. Planned improvements are
431	project state, tracked in the project's `TODO.md` —
432	this protocol describes only the stable mechanism.
433	
434	### .claude cadence
435	
436	**Cadence**: one push = one bot-repo commit, paired
437	with every work-repo commit in that push.
438	
439	The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
440	across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
441	snapshots, `jj describe`, and the squash-push fold, so
442	work-repo `ochid:` trailers resolve.
443	
444	`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
445	on `main`, regardless of the work-repo bookmark. **Do
446	not create or maintain bot-repo bookmarks that mirror
447	work-repo branches** — risks the bot steering session
448	pushes to the wrong remote ref.
449	
450	Ending a session: if the user runs `/exit` there will be
451	session information created, which we don't worry about.
452	The user can close the terminal instead and `@` will
453	remain empty.
454	
455	### Bot communication at the reviews
456	
457	Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
458	"Checkpoint N", etc.):
459	
460	- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
461	  "Work complete. Please review."
462	- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
463	  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
464	  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
465	  --body ...` invocation by default.
466	- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
467	  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
468	  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
469	  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.
470	
471	### After push or squash-push: stop and wait
472	
473	After a **push** (crossing the remote boundary, by hand or
474	via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — whose last stage publishes
475	the bot repo too) or a manual **squash-push** on the bot
476	repo, stop for the turn: no next step, edit, tool call, or
477	text output until the user directs otherwise. **Even when
478	the next step seems obvious — wait.**
479	
480	- **Scope**: the stop follows the user's directive, not the
481	  push. A standing directive covering more work ("finish
482	  the remaining ladder commits on your own") makes an
483	  intermediate push just a step; the hard stop lands on the
484	  turn's *final* push.
485	- **Why**: the bot repo is a live journal — everything after
486	  the invocation (its own record, closing words) lands in
487	  `@` as a trailing tail. Between delegated pushes the tail
488	  rides into the next cycle's bot commit; the final push's
489	  tail has no next commit, and the bot's own squash-push is
490	  itself session data (`@` refills immediately), so only the
491	  user, after the turn, can capture it
492	  (`vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude`).
493	- **Silence**: put all closing words *before* the final
494	  push. The harness rejects an empty turn, so it may force a
495	  visible token after the tool returns; if so, emit a bare
496	  acknowledgment only (e.g. "landed") — never a summary,
497	  verification, or next-step offer. There is no "harmless"
498	  closing line after the push — a known slip.
499	- **Flush**: when the user wants `@` empty (no tail), they
500	  run `vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude` after the bot goes
501	  quiet — it flushes all bot session information into the
502	  published commit. Repeat if new writes land (see
503	  [Recovery](#recovery)).
504	
505	### Recovery
506	
507	- **If push exits before its last stage** — `push-work`
508	  succeeded but the bot-repo publish didn't run
509	  (`squash-push-bot` in `vc-x1 push --status` / `--from`
510	  stage names) — run the squash+push by hand:
511	
512	  ```
513	  vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude
514	  ```
515	
516	  It runs in-process, so a failure is a visible non-zero
517	  exit — no log file to chase.
518	- **Run squash-push again if `@` is non-empty** after a
519	  pass (also desirable after extra activity by the bot's
520	  agents).
521	  - Why: the bot keeps writing session data while the
522	    command runs — the invocation's own record plus any
523	    closing response land after the squash.
524	  - Safe to repeat: bot session data is append-only, so a
525	    re-run never conflicts or overwrites. (This could
526	    change; it is not under the user's control.)
527	  - No guarantees: events outside the bot's control can leave
528	    `@` non-empty — e.g. the bot's back end may decide to
529	    squash/consolidate session data, which can take minutes
530	    and land after the pass. The remedy is the same: just
531	    run squash-push again. This is why a single pass is never
532	    guaranteed to leave `@` empty.
533	- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
534	  recovery — `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
535	  <bookmark> --restart` — otherwise push resumes from a
536	  stale stage.
537	- **Late work-repo tweak after the work-repo push succeeded**
538	  (e.g. updating AGENTS.md or memory) requires `jj
539	  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a
540	  remote rewrite and needs explicit approval like any
541	  push.
542	
543	## Iterative work
544	
545	When work for a single commit (the **target**) benefits
546	from incremental review, loop:
547	
548	1. `jj new -R .` — fresh empty `@` on top of the target.
549	2. Make the next round of changes.
550	3. User reviews the round (see
551	   [Reviewing changes](#reviewing-changes)).
552	4. `jj squash -R .` folds into the target and creates a
553	   new empty `@`.
554	5. If not done, go to step 2.
555	
556	Same jj mechanics as a
557	[sub-cycle ladder](#sub-cycle-ladders), but at
558	single-commit scope — the version
559	doesn't change.
560	
561	## Sub-cycle ladders
562	
563	When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
564	[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
565	commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and
566	**collapse into the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
567	parent cycle continues. Ladder commits are scratch —
568	for review and bisection only.
569	
570	### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder
571	
572	For each Work commit in the ladder:
573	
574	1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
575	2. Do the commit's work.
576	3. Run the fast validation (Rust example: `cargo test
577	   --bins`). **Non-negotiable** — for code, build and clippy
578	   alone miss regressions until a later commit runs the full
579	   suite, raising bisection cost.
580	4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — working title
581	   only; the sub-cycle Close-out collects everything
582	   into one final commit.
583	
584	### Navigating the ladder
585	
586	Common moves:
587	
588	- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` — see the whole ladder
589	  from its base.
590	- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any ladder
591	  commit by chid prefix; useful for bisection.
592	- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
593	- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
594	  isolation.
595	
596	Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
597	descendants auto-rebase.
598	
599	### Close-out: squash the ladder
600	
601	When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:
602	
603	```
604	jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
605	```
606	
607	`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
608	keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
609	After squash, history is linear: `<base> → @`;
610	intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.
611	
612	Then `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for any other commit.
613	
614	For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
615	when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.
616	
617	### Recovery
618	
619	If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
620	prior commits:
621	
622	- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
623	  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same
624	  parent.
625	- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
626	  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
627	  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
628	- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows
629	  the op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts
630	  to that point. Full undo — removes *all* ladder work
631	  after the chosen op. Use only to start over.
632	
633	# References
634	
635	- [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets) — revset primitives
636	  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
637	- The per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate exists because a
638	  regression introduced in an early ladder commit can go
639	  uncaught until a later commit runs the full suite, raising
640	  bisection cost.
641
